Name: RateDistortionOptimalPruning
Title: Rate-Distortion Optimal Pruning for Deep Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the optimal pruning strategy for deep neural networks can be derived from rate-distortion theory, where the goal is to minimize the distortion (loss in performance) for a given rate (number of retained parameters).
Related Work: Recent works have explored the application of rate-distortion theory to model compression and pruning [1, 2, 3, 7, 8]. However, these works either focus on specific aspects of the problem, such as the impact of side information [9] or the use of specific compression techniques [4, 5], or they do not provide a comprehensive framework for deriving optimal pruning policies. Our work aims to bridge this gap by formulating the pruning problem as a rate-distortion optimization problem and deriving a principled method for determining the optimal number of parameters to retain.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for model pruning in deep neural networks using rate-distortion theory. Our approach formulates the pruning problem as an optimization problem where the goal is to minimize the distortion (loss in performance) for a given rate (number of retained parameters). By leveraging information-theoretic metrics such as mutual information and entropy, we derive a principled method for determining the optimal pruning policy. We validate our approach on several benchmark datasets and architectures, demonstrating that it consistently outperforms existing heuristic pruning methods in terms of the rate-distortion trade-off. Our theoretical analysis provides a clear understanding of the compression-accuracy trade-off and offers insights into the optimal allocation of model parameters.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Rate-Distortion Curve Analysis - We will compute the rate-distortion curve for various pruning policies, including our proposed method and several heuristic baselines. The rate will be defined as the number of retained parameters, and the distortion will be the validation loss. We will use this to evaluate the effectiveness of each policy in terms of the trade-off between compression and performance.

Experiment 2: Sensitivity Analysis - We will perform a sensitivity analysis to understand how the choice of mutual information estimator and entropy threshold affects the rate-distortion performance. This will help us identify the most robust and reliable settings for our method.

Experiment 3: Generalization Performance - We will compare the generalization performance of models pruned using our method with those pruned using existing heuristic methods. This will involve training the pruned models on a training set and evaluating their performance on a separate validation and test set.

Experiment 4: Real-World Application - We will apply our pruning method to a real-world dataset and architecture, such as a large-scale image classification task on ImageNet, to demonstrate its practical effectiveness and scalability.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The choice of mutual information estimator and entropy threshold can significantly impact the performance of the pruning policy. Careful calibration and validation will be required to ensure robustness.
The computational complexity of computing mutual information and entropy for large models may be high. We will need to explore efficient approximation methods to make the approach feasible.
The derived pruning policy may not always generalize well to different datasets or architectures. We will need to validate the method on a diverse set of tasks to ensure its broad applicability.
